questions.question: What is the healthiest default interpretation of an interviewer interruption?
options: It is usually a signal about where they want more evidence or sharper trade-off reasoning.
It means your design has already failed completely.
It means you should stop explaining architecture altogether.
It means the interviewer prefers coding to design.
questions.explanation: Interruptions usually reveal what signal the interviewer wants next.

questions.question: Why is it useful to classify the interviewer move before answering?
options: It helps you respond at the right depth without losing the thread of the overall answer.
It guarantees the interviewer will agree with your response.
It lets you ignore the question if it is inconvenient.
It replaces the need for clarification and sizing.
questions.explanation: Classification helps you decide whether the move is a probe, redirect, or redesign trigger.

questions.question: What is the strongest response pattern after an interruption?
options: Acknowledge it, state what changes, answer tightly, and reconnect to the main structure.
Defend the original answer until the interviewer withdraws the question.
Restart the entire design from the beginning.
Switch fully into implementation details.
questions.explanation: The goal is adaptation without losing coherence.

questions.question: If the interviewer asks for trade-offs, what should you usually do first?
options: Name two plausible options and explain what each buys and costs.
Add more components so the design seems more complete.
Repeat the same architecture with stronger confidence.
Avoid answering until all implementation details are known.
questions.explanation: Trade-off probes are about judgment between alternatives.

questions.question: Which behavior most often causes an interruption to derail the interview?
options: Never returning to the original flow after answering the probe.
Using a short pause before speaking.
Restating the changed assumption clearly.
Keeping the response under one minute.
questions.explanation: A good response is only half the job; you also need to reconnect.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with very concise interruption responses?
options: They preserve pace, but may leave nuance implicit unless you reconnect carefully.
They automatically reduce system reliability.
They prevent any deep dives later in the interview.
They remove the need for observability discussion.
questions.explanation: Concise answers are efficient, but they need enough framing to stay clear.

questions.question: Which signal best measures whether interruption handling is improving in practice?
options: Time to recover plus whether the answer returns to a clear structure afterward.
How many services were renamed during the interruption.
The length of every response in words.
How many times the candidate said 'it depends'.
questions.explanation: Recovery quality matters more than stylistic quirks.

questions.question: If the interviewer challenges one of your assumptions, what is the best first move?
options: Surface the assumption explicitly and explain how the design changes if the new assumption is true.
Insist the original assumption was standard and continue.
Switch to a completely different product example.
Skip directly to your wrap-up.
questions.explanation: Making the assumption visible is the foundation of a strong adaptation.
